longCommonName: Apolipoprotein A1 [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Main protein component of HDL cholesterol particles.
info.scientific: Apolipoprotein A-I (ApoA-I) is the major structural and functional protein component of high-density lipoprotein (HDL) particles, comprising approximately 70% of HDL protein mass. It activates lecithin-cholesterol acyltransferase (LCAT), which esterifies free cholesterol on HDL, and mediates cholesterol efflux from peripheral cells via the ABCA1 transporter, driving reverse cholesterol transport. Low ApoA-I levels are associated with increased cardiovascular risk, and the ApoB/ApoA-I ratio is considered one of the strongest lipid-related predictors of myocardial infarction. ApoA-I measurement provides additional cardiovascular risk information beyond HDL-C, particularly in patients with discordant HDL-C and HDL particle levels.
info.why: Assesses cardiovascular protection; the ApoB/ApoA1 ratio is a strong predictor of heart disease risk.

longCommonName: Lipoprotein a [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Genetically determined lipoprotein particle that promotes atherosclerosis and blood clotting.
info.scientific: Lipoprotein(a) [Lp(a)] is a modified LDL particle in which apolipoprotein B-100 is covalently linked via a disulfide bond to apolipoprotein(a), a highly polymorphic glycoprotein with structural homology to plasminogen. Lp(a) levels are primarily genetically determined by the LPA gene and are minimally affected by diet, exercise, or most lipid-lowering drugs. Elevated Lp(a) is an independent, causal risk factor for atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, aortic valve stenosis, and thrombosis due to its dual pro-atherogenic and anti-fibrinolytic properties. Because Lp(a) levels are stable throughout life, a single measurement is generally sufficient for cardiovascular risk stratification.
info.why: Identifies inherited cardiovascular risk not captured by standard lipid panels.
info.frequency: Once in a lifetime (genetically determined); repeat only if result was borderline

longCommonName: C reactive protein [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Acute-phase protein produced by the liver that rises rapidly with inflammation or infection.
info.scientific: C-reactive protein (CRP) is a pentameric acute-phase protein synthesized by hepatocytes in response to interleukin-6 (IL-6) signaling during inflammation, infection, and tissue injury. CRP binds to phosphocholine on the surface of damaged cells and pathogens, activating the classical complement pathway and facilitating phagocytosis. Serum CRP can increase dramatically (up to 1000-fold) within 6-8 hours of an acute inflammatory stimulus and has a short half-life of approximately 19 hours, making it a responsive marker of disease activity. CRP is used clinically to detect and monitor bacterial infections, autoimmune flares, postoperative complications, and to guide antibiotic therapy.
info.why: Detects and monitors acute inflammation, infection, and tissue injury.

longCommonName: C reactive protein [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma by High sensitivity method

info.description: Highly sensitive measurement of CRP that detects low-grade chronic inflammation.
info.scientific: High-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hs-CRP) is measured using an assay optimized to detect very low concentrations of CRP (typically 0.1-10 mg/L), enabling the detection of low-grade chronic inflammation not captured by standard CRP assays. It is produced by hepatocytes in response to pro-inflammatory cytokines, particularly IL-6. Hs-CRP has been validated as an independent predictor of cardiovascular events, including myocardial infarction and stroke, reflecting the role of vascular inflammation in atherosclerosis. The American Heart Association stratifies cardiovascular risk based on hs-CRP levels: less than 1 mg/L (low risk), 1-3 mg/L (moderate risk), and greater than 3 mg/L (high risk).
info.why: Assesses cardiovascular disease risk related to vascular inflammation.
info.frequency: Annually, or as clinically indicated for cardiovascular risk stratification

longCommonName: Interleukin 6 [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Pro-inflammatory cytokine produced by immune cells during infection and tissue injury.
info.scientific: Interleukin-6 (IL-6) is a pleiotropic cytokine produced by monocytes, macrophages, T-cells, endothelial cells, and fibroblasts that plays a central role in the acute-phase inflammatory response and immune regulation. IL-6 signals through both classical membrane-bound receptor (anti-inflammatory) and trans-signaling (pro-inflammatory) pathways and is the primary hepatic stimulus for acute-phase protein production including CRP and fibrinogen. Elevated serum IL-6 is found in sepsis, autoimmune diseases, chronic inflammatory conditions, and certain malignancies. IL-6 is a therapeutic target in conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis and cytokine release syndrome, with anti-IL-6 receptor antibodies (tocilizumab) used in clinical practice.
info.why: Evaluates systemic inflammation and monitors autoimmune or inflammatory conditions.

longCommonName: Tumor necrosis factor alpha [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Key pro-inflammatory cytokine involved in systemic inflammation and immune regulation.
info.scientific: Tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNF-alpha) is a 17-kDa pro-inflammatory cytokine produced primarily by activated macrophages, T-lymphocytes, and natural killer cells, existing as both a transmembrane and soluble homotrimer. It signals through TNF receptor 1 (ubiquitous) and TNF receptor 2 (immune cells) to activate NF-kB, MAPK, and caspase-mediated apoptotic pathways. TNF-alpha is a master regulator of the inflammatory cascade, inducing the production of other pro-inflammatory cytokines, chemokines, and adhesion molecules. Chronically elevated TNF-alpha is implicated in the pathogenesis of rheumatoid arthritis, inflammatory bowel disease, psoriasis, and cachexia, and is the target of anti-TNF biologic therapies.
info.why: Monitors chronic inflammatory and autoimmune diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis.

longCommonName: Oxidized LDL [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: LDL cholesterol that has been damaged by oxidation, making it more atherogenic.
info.scientific: Oxidized LDL (oxLDL) refers to LDL particles that have undergone lipid peroxidation and protein modification by reactive oxygen species, making them highly atherogenic. Once oxidized, LDL is no longer recognized by the normal LDL receptor but is instead taken up by scavenger receptors (CD36, SR-A) on macrophages in an unregulated manner, leading to foam cell formation and fatty streak development. OxLDL also promotes endothelial dysfunction, smooth muscle cell proliferation, and platelet aggregation, contributing to plaque instability. Measurement of circulating oxLDL provides information about oxidative stress burden and may offer incremental cardiovascular risk prediction beyond standard lipid parameters.
info.why: Assesses oxidative stress and provides additional cardiovascular risk information.

longCommonName: Leptin [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Hormone produced by fat cells that signals satiety and regulates energy balance.
info.scientific: Leptin is a 16-kDa peptide hormone encoded by the LEP (ob) gene and produced primarily by white adipose tissue in proportion to fat mass. It acts on leptin receptors (Ob-R) in the hypothalamus to suppress appetite, increase energy expenditure, and regulate neuroendocrine function, serving as a key adiposity signal in the homeostatic control of body weight. Obesity is typically associated with elevated leptin levels and leptin resistance, while congenital leptin deficiency causes severe early-onset obesity that is treatable with exogenous leptin. Leptin also plays roles in reproductive function, immune modulation, bone metabolism, and angiogenesis.
info.why: Evaluates obesity-related metabolic dysfunction and rare congenital leptin deficiency.

longCommonName: Adiponectin [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Hormone secreted by fat cells that enhances insulin sensitivity and has anti-inflammatory effects.
info.scientific: Adiponectin is a 30-kDa adipokine encoded by the ADIPOQ gene and secreted exclusively by adipocytes, circulating in plasma as trimers, hexamers, and high-molecular-weight (HMW) multimers. Unlike most adipokines, adiponectin levels are inversely correlated with adiposity, with lower levels found in obesity, type 2 diabetes, and metabolic syndrome. Adiponectin enhances insulin sensitivity through activation of AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK) and peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor alpha (PPAR-alpha), and exerts anti-inflammatory and anti-atherogenic effects on the vascular endothelium. Low adiponectin is independently associated with increased risk of type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease.
info.why: Assesses metabolic health; low levels are associated with insulin resistance and cardiovascular risk.

longCommonName: Homocysteine [Moles/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Amino acid whose elevated levels damage blood vessel walls and promote clotting.
info.scientific: Homocysteine is a sulfur-containing amino acid formed as an intermediate in the metabolism of methionine, requiring folate, vitamin B12, and vitamin B6 as cofactors for its remethylation and transsulfuration pathways. Elevated plasma homocysteine (hyperhomocysteinemia) is an independent risk factor for atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, venous thromboembolism, and stroke, as it promotes endothelial dysfunction, oxidative stress, and smooth muscle cell proliferation. Genetic causes of severe hyperhomocysteinemia include homozygous cystathionine beta-synthase deficiency, while milder elevations commonly result from folate or vitamin B12 deficiency, renal insufficiency, or the MTHFR C677T polymorphism. Treatment with B-vitamin supplementation effectively lowers homocysteine levels.
info.why: Identifies cardiovascular and stroke risk and screens for B-vitamin or folate deficiency.

longCommonName: Fibrinogen [Mass/volume] in Platelet poor plasma by Coagulation assay

info.description: Blood clotting protein converted to fibrin to form clots, also an acute-phase reactant.
info.scientific: Fibrinogen (Factor I) is a 340-kDa hexameric glycoprotein composed of two sets of three polypeptide chains (alpha, beta, gamma) synthesized by hepatocytes and released into plasma at concentrations of 200-400 mg/dL. It is the precursor to fibrin, cleaved by thrombin at the final step of the coagulation cascade to form fibrin monomers that polymerize into the structural meshwork of a blood clot. Fibrinogen is also a major acute-phase reactant whose levels increase with inflammation, infection, and tissue injury. Elevated fibrinogen is an independent risk factor for cardiovascular disease and thrombosis, while decreased levels occur in disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC), liver disease, and congenital afibrinogenemia.
info.why: Evaluates bleeding disorders, clotting risk, and systemic inflammation.

longCommonName: Sex hormone binding globulin [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Liver-produced protein that binds and transports sex hormones, regulating their bioavailability.
info.scientific: Sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG) is a homodimeric glycoprotein synthesized by hepatocytes that binds and transports sex steroids, primarily testosterone and estradiol, in the circulation. By sequestering these hormones, SHBG regulates their bioavailability to target tissues, with only the unbound (free) and loosely albumin-bound fractions considered biologically active. SHBG production is stimulated by estrogen, thyroxine, and hepatic dysfunction, and suppressed by insulin, androgens, obesity, and growth hormone. SHBG measurement is essential for calculating free and bioavailable testosterone and for interpreting total testosterone levels in the context of conditions that alter SHBG concentrations.
info.why: Helps interpret testosterone and estrogen levels and assess androgen excess or deficiency.

longCommonName: Estradiol [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Primary female sex hormone essential for reproductive function and bone health.
info.scientific: Estradiol (17-beta-estradiol, E2) is the most potent naturally occurring estrogen, a C18 steroid hormone synthesized primarily by ovarian granulosa cells in premenopausal women via aromatization of testosterone, with smaller contributions from the adrenal cortex and peripheral adipose tissue. It acts through nuclear estrogen receptors alpha and beta to regulate the menstrual cycle, endometrial proliferation, breast development, bone metabolism, cardiovascular function, and central nervous system activity. Estradiol measurement is essential for evaluating ovarian function, menstrual irregularities, infertility, menopausal status, and for monitoring assisted reproduction protocols and anti-estrogen therapy. In males, estradiol is produced by peripheral aromatization and contributes to bone health and cardiovascular protection.
info.why: Evaluates ovarian function, menopause, fertility issues, and gynecomastia in men.

longCommonName: Dihydrotestosterone [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Potent androgen derived from testosterone, active in skin, hair follicles, and the prostate.
info.scientific: Dihydrotestosterone (DHT) is a potent 5-alpha-reduced androgen formed from testosterone by the enzyme 5-alpha-reductase (types I and II) in peripheral target tissues including the prostate, skin, and hair follicles. DHT has approximately 2-3 times greater binding affinity for the androgen receptor than testosterone and is the principal androgen mediating prostate growth, sebaceous gland activity, male pattern hair loss, and external genital virilization during fetal development. Elevated DHT is implicated in benign prostatic hyperplasia, androgenetic alopecia, and hirsutism, and is the pharmacologic target of 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors (finasteride, dutasteride). DHT measurement is useful in evaluating androgen excess syndromes and monitoring 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor therapy.
info.why: Evaluates androgen-related conditions such as hair loss, hirsutism, and prostate enlargement.

longCommonName: Dehydroepiandrosterone sulfate [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Adrenal androgen precursor that serves as a reservoir for sex hormone production.
info.scientific: Dehydroepiandrosterone sulfate (DHEA-S) is a 19-carbon adrenal androgen precursor produced almost exclusively by the zona reticularis of the adrenal cortex, making it a specific marker of adrenal androgen production. It is the most abundant circulating steroid hormone and serves as a reservoir for the peripheral conversion to more potent androgens (testosterone, DHT) and estrogens. DHEA-S levels exhibit a characteristic age-related pattern, peaking in early adulthood and declining progressively with aging (adrenopause). Elevated DHEA-S suggests adrenal androgen excess from congenital adrenal hyperplasia, adrenal tumors, or Cushing syndrome, while low levels may indicate adrenal insufficiency or hypopituitarism.
info.why: Evaluates adrenal gland function and investigates androgen excess or adrenal tumors.

longCommonName: Thiamine [Mass/volume] in Blood

info.description: Water-soluble vitamin essential for energy metabolism and nervous system function.
info.scientific: Thiamine (vitamin B1) is a water-soluble vitamin whose active form, thiamine pyrophosphate (TPP), serves as an essential coenzyme for pyruvate dehydrogenase, alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase, branched-chain alpha-keto acid dehydrogenase, and transketolase in the pentose phosphate pathway. These enzymes are critical for carbohydrate metabolism, the citric acid cycle, ATP production, and nucleotide synthesis. Thiamine deficiency results in beriberi (wet beriberi with heart failure; dry beriberi with peripheral neuropathy) and Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome in the central nervous system. Deficiency is most common in chronic alcoholism, malnutrition, and conditions with increased metabolic demand.
info.why: Detects deficiency that can cause beriberi or Wernicke encephalopathy.

longCommonName: Pyridoxal phosphate [Mass/volume] in Plasma

info.description: Active form of vitamin B6 involved in amino acid metabolism and neurotransmitter synthesis.
info.scientific: Pyridoxal 5'-phosphate (PLP) is the metabolically active coenzyme form of vitamin B6, involved in over 100 enzymatic reactions primarily in amino acid metabolism, including transamination, decarboxylation, and racemization. It is also required for the synthesis of neurotransmitters (serotonin, dopamine, GABA, norepinephrine), heme (via delta-aminolevulinic acid synthase), and sphingolipids. PLP plays a role in homocysteine metabolism as a coenzyme for cystathionine beta-synthase in the transsulfuration pathway. Deficiency manifests as microcytic sideroblastic anemia, peripheral neuropathy, seizures (especially in neonates), dermatitis, and glossitis, and may cause elevated homocysteine levels.
info.why: Detects deficiency associated with peripheral neuropathy and anemia.

longCommonName: Folate [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: B-vitamin essential for DNA synthesis, red blood cell formation, and fetal development.
info.scientific: Folate (vitamin B9) comprises a family of pteroylglutamic acid derivatives that function as one-carbon carriers essential for purine and thymidylate synthesis, DNA methylation, and the remethylation of homocysteine to methionine via methionine synthase (vitamin B12-dependent). Folate is obtained from dietary sources (leafy greens, legumes, fortified cereals) and is absorbed in the proximal jejunum. Deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia due to impaired DNA synthesis, elevated homocysteine levels increasing cardiovascular risk, and neural tube defects in developing fetuses. Serum folate reflects recent dietary intake, while red blood cell folate better represents tissue stores over the preceding 2-3 months.
info.why: Detects folate deficiency causing megaloblastic anemia and elevated homocysteine.

longCommonName: Cobalamin [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Vitamin essential for nerve function, DNA synthesis, and red blood cell production.
info.scientific: Cobalamin (vitamin B12) is a cobalt-containing water-soluble vitamin that functions as a coenzyme for methionine synthase (converting homocysteine to methionine) and methylmalonyl-CoA mutase (converting methylmalonyl-CoA to succinyl-CoA). Absorption requires gastric intrinsic factor and an intact ileum. Vitamin B12 deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia indistinguishable from folate deficiency, but uniquely also produces neurological damage (subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord) due to impaired myelin synthesis. Common causes include pernicious anemia (autoimmune destruction of parietal cells), gastrectomy, ileal resection, and strict vegan diets, and deficiency can be confirmed by elevated methylmalonic acid and homocysteine.
info.why: Detects deficiency causing megaloblastic anemia, neuropathy, and cognitive impairment.

longCommonName: 25-Hydroxyvitamin D [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Main circulating form of vitamin D reflecting overall body stores.
info.scientific: 25-Hydroxyvitamin D [25(OH)D] is the major circulating metabolite of vitamin D, formed by hepatic 25-hydroxylation of both cholecalciferol (vitamin D3, synthesized in skin or dietary) and ergocalciferol (vitamin D2, dietary). It is the best indicator of overall vitamin D status, reflecting combined contributions from sunlight exposure, diet, and supplements. 25(OH)D is subsequently converted to the biologically active form, 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D [calcitriol], in the kidneys under regulation by parathyroid hormone. Deficiency (generally below 20 ng/mL) leads to impaired calcium absorption, secondary hyperparathyroidism, osteomalacia in adults, and rickets in children, and has been associated with increased risk of autoimmune diseases, infections, and cardiovascular events.
info.why: Detects vitamin D deficiency linked to bone loss, immune dysfunction, and muscle weakness.
info.frequency: Annually; every 3 months when supplementing to reach target levels

longCommonName: Calcium [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Mineral essential for bone structure, muscle contraction, nerve function, and blood clotting.
info.scientific: Calcium is the most abundant mineral in the human body, with approximately 99% stored in bone and teeth as hydroxyapatite crystals, and the remaining 1% in blood and soft tissues. In serum, calcium exists in three forms: protein-bound (primarily to albumin, ~40%), complexed with anions (~10%), and free ionized (~50%), the latter being the physiologically active fraction. Calcium is essential for bone mineralization, muscle contraction, nerve impulse transmission, blood coagulation, and intracellular signaling. Serum calcium homeostasis is tightly regulated by parathyroid hormone, calcitonin, and 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D acting on bone, kidney, and intestine.
info.why: Screens for parathyroid disorders, bone disease, and kidney problems.

longCommonName: Calcium.ionized [Moles/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Free, biologically active form of calcium not bound to proteins.
info.scientific: Ionized calcium (free calcium) represents the physiologically active fraction of total serum calcium that is not bound to proteins or complexed with anions. It accounts for approximately 50% of total calcium and is the form directly involved in neuromuscular excitability, cardiac contractility, coagulation, and cellular signaling through calcium-sensing receptors. Ionized calcium measurement is preferred over total calcium when albumin levels are abnormal, in critically ill patients, during massive transfusion (citrate binding), and in the evaluation of acid-base disturbances that alter protein binding. Both parathyroid hormone secretion and the clinical symptoms of hypocalcemia and hypercalcemia correlate with ionized rather than total calcium concentration.
info.why: Provides a more accurate assessment of calcium status when albumin levels are abnormal.

longCommonName: Chloride [Moles/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Electrolyte that helps maintain fluid balance and acid-base equilibrium.
info.scientific: Chloride is the major extracellular anion, working in concert with sodium to maintain plasma osmolality, fluid distribution, and electrical neutrality. It is essential for gastric acid (HCl) production, acid-base balance through the chloride shift in erythrocytes, and renal tubular function. Chloride is absorbed in the gastrointestinal tract and excreted primarily by the kidneys, with reabsorption closely linked to sodium in the proximal tubule and the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle. Abnormalities in serum chloride are often evaluated in the context of acid-base disorders: hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis indicates a normal anion gap acidosis, while hypochloremia may accompany metabolic alkalosis from vomiting or diuretic use.
info.why: Detects acid-base disorders and electrolyte imbalances.

longCommonName: Phosphate [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Mineral essential for bone structure, energy production, and cell membrane integrity.
info.scientific: Phosphorus (measured as inorganic phosphate in serum) is the second most abundant mineral in the body, with approximately 85% in bone and teeth, 14% in soft tissues, and less than 1% in extracellular fluid. It is essential for bone mineralization as a component of hydroxyapatite, energy metabolism (ATP, creatine phosphate), nucleic acid structure, cell membrane phospholipids, and acid-base buffering. Serum phosphate is regulated by parathyroid hormone (which promotes renal excretion), fibroblast growth factor 23 (FGF23), and 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D (which enhances intestinal absorption). Hyperphosphatemia occurs in chronic kidney disease and hypoparathyroidism, while hypophosphatemia is seen in refeeding syndrome, hyperparathyroidism, and vitamin D deficiency.
info.why: Evaluates bone metabolism, parathyroid function, and kidney disease.

longCommonName: Magnesium [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Mineral involved in over 300 enzymatic reactions including energy production and muscle function.
info.scientific: Magnesium is the fourth most abundant cation in the body and the second most abundant intracellular cation, with approximately 60% stored in bone, 39% in intracellular compartments, and only 1% in extracellular fluid. It is an essential cofactor for over 300 enzymatic reactions, including those involving ATP utilization, protein and nucleic acid synthesis, ion channel regulation, and neuromuscular function. Magnesium deficiency can cause neuromuscular irritability (tremor, tetany, seizures), cardiac arrhythmias, hypokalemia, and hypocalcemia resistant to correction until magnesium is repleted. Serum magnesium may not accurately reflect total body stores since it represents less than 1% of total magnesium.
info.why: Detects deficiency causing muscle cramps, arrhythmias, and fatigue.

longCommonName: Magnesium [Mass/volume] in Red Blood Cells

info.description: Intracellular magnesium level measured in red blood cells, reflecting longer-term magnesium status.
info.scientific: Erythrocyte (red blood cell) magnesium measures the intracellular magnesium content within red blood cells, providing a more representative assessment of tissue and cellular magnesium stores compared to serum magnesium. Since only about 1% of total body magnesium resides in the extracellular space, serum levels can remain normal despite significant intracellular depletion. RBC magnesium reflects magnesium status over the preceding 120-day erythrocyte lifespan, analogous to how HbA1c reflects glucose exposure. This measurement is particularly useful in patients with symptoms suggestive of magnesium deficiency (muscle cramps, fatigue, arrhythmias) despite normal serum magnesium levels.
info.why: Provides a more accurate picture of tissue magnesium stores than serum magnesium alone.

longCommonName: Parathyrin.intact [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Hormone that regulates calcium and phosphorus levels by acting on bones, kidneys, and gut.
info.scientific: Parathyroid hormone (PTH) is an 84-amino acid polypeptide secreted by the chief cells of the parathyroid glands in response to decreasing ionized calcium levels detected by the calcium-sensing receptor (CaSR). PTH acts to raise serum calcium through three mechanisms: stimulating osteoclast-mediated bone resorption, increasing renal tubular calcium reabsorption while promoting phosphate excretion, and enhancing renal 1-alpha-hydroxylase activity to increase 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D production. Intact PTH measurement is essential for the differential diagnosis of hypercalcemia (primary hyperparathyroidism versus malignancy-associated hypercalcemia) and hypocalcemia (hypoparathyroidism versus vitamin D deficiency). PTH also guides the management of secondary hyperparathyroidism in chronic kidney disease.
info.why: Diagnoses hyperparathyroidism, hypoparathyroidism, and evaluates calcium metabolism disorders.

longCommonName: Hematocrit [Volume Fraction] of Blood by Automated count

info.description: How much of your blood is actually red blood cells versus liquid plasma — think of it as the "thickness" of your blood. A low hematocrit confirms anemia; a high one means your blood is more viscous, which can strain the heart and increase clot risk. It's also sensitive to hydration — dehydration artificially inflates it, while over-hydration dilutes it.
info.scientific: Hematocrit is the volume percentage of red blood cells in whole blood, determined by automated hematology analyzers from the product of the red blood cell count and mean corpuscular volume (MCV). It reflects the oxygen-carrying capacity of the blood and the balance between red cell production, destruction, and plasma volume. Decreased hematocrit indicates anemia from blood loss, hemolysis, nutritional deficiency, or bone marrow failure, while elevated hematocrit occurs in polycythemia vera, chronic hypoxia, dehydration, and erythropoietin-secreting tumors. Hematocrit is used clinically to guide transfusion decisions, monitor fluid status, and assess the severity of anemia.
info.why: Detects anemia, dehydration, and polycythemia.

longCommonName: Iron [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Essential mineral required for hemoglobin synthesis and oxygen transport.
info.scientific: Serum iron measures the concentration of ferric iron (Fe3+) bound to transferrin in the circulation, representing iron in transit between sites of absorption (duodenum), storage (liver, macrophages), and utilization (bone marrow erythroid precursors). Serum iron exhibits significant diurnal variation (higher in the morning) and is affected by recent dietary iron intake, making it an imperfect standalone marker. It is most informative when interpreted alongside ferritin, TIBC, and transferrin saturation to characterize iron status. Low serum iron is seen in iron deficiency anemia, anemia of chronic disease, and acute-phase responses, while elevated levels occur in hemochromatosis, iron overload, and acute hepatitis.
info.why: Evaluates iron deficiency or overload and helps diagnose anemia.

longCommonName: Ferritin [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Protein that stores iron inside cells, reflecting total body iron reserves.
info.scientific: Ferritin is a ubiquitous intracellular protein composed of 24 subunits (H and L chains) that form a hollow spherical shell capable of storing up to 4,500 iron atoms as ferric oxyhydroxide. A small amount of ferritin is released into the circulation, and serum ferritin concentration directly correlates with total body iron stores under non-inflammatory conditions. Ferritin is the most sensitive and specific single test for iron deficiency, with levels below 15 ng/mL being virtually diagnostic. However, as an acute-phase reactant, ferritin is elevated in inflammation, infection, liver disease, and malignancy independent of iron status, requiring concurrent assessment of transferrin saturation and CRP for accurate interpretation.
info.why: Best single test for diagnosing iron deficiency; also elevated in inflammation and iron overload.

longCommonName: Iron binding capacity [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Measures the blood's total capacity to bind iron via transferrin.
info.scientific: Total iron-binding capacity (TIBC) measures the maximum amount of iron that serum transferrin can bind, serving as an indirect measure of transferrin concentration. In iron deficiency, hepatic transferrin synthesis increases to maximize iron capture, resulting in elevated TIBC, while in iron overload, chronic disease, and malnutrition, TIBC is decreased. TIBC is used in conjunction with serum iron to calculate transferrin saturation (serum iron/TIBC x 100), which is one of the most useful parameters for distinguishing iron deficiency anemia (low saturation) from iron overload states such as hemochromatosis (high saturation). TIBC is less affected by acute-phase responses than ferritin, making it a useful complementary marker.
info.why: Helps distinguish between iron deficiency anemia and other causes of abnormal iron levels.

longCommonName: Iron binding capacity.unsaturated [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: The reserve iron-binding capacity of transferrin not yet occupied by iron.
info.scientific: Unsaturated iron-binding capacity (UIBC) represents the reserve iron-binding capacity of transferrin that is not currently occupied by iron. It is calculated as the difference between TIBC and serum iron (UIBC = TIBC - serum iron), or measured directly by saturating transferrin with excess iron and quantifying the unbound iron. Elevated UIBC indicates increased available transferrin binding sites, as seen in iron deficiency, while decreased UIBC suggests iron overload or saturation of transferrin. Together with serum iron, UIBC allows calculation of TIBC and transferrin saturation without separate measurement, providing an efficient approach to comprehensive iron status assessment.
info.why: Helps calculate transferrin saturation and assess iron status.

longCommonName: Transferrin [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Iron-transport protein produced by the liver that carries iron through the bloodstream.
info.scientific: Transferrin is an 80-kDa beta-globulin glycoprotein synthesized primarily by the liver, with each molecule containing two high-affinity iron-binding sites for ferric iron (Fe3+). It is the principal plasma iron transport protein, delivering iron from sites of absorption and storage to erythroid precursors and other cells expressing transferrin receptors. Transferrin synthesis is inversely regulated by iron status: production increases in iron deficiency and decreases in iron overload. As a negative acute-phase reactant, transferrin levels decrease during inflammation and infection. Transferrin measurement is used alongside serum iron to calculate transferrin saturation and to help differentiate iron deficiency from anemia of chronic disease.
info.why: Evaluates iron metabolism and helps distinguish types of anemia.

longCommonName: Iron saturation [Mass Fraction] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Percentage of transferrin binding sites occupied by iron.
info.scientific: Transferrin saturation (TSAT) is the calculated percentage of available transferrin iron-binding sites that are occupied by iron, derived from the formula: serum iron divided by TIBC (or transferrin concentration) multiplied by 100. It is one of the most clinically useful iron parameters for distinguishing iron deficiency (TSAT typically below 20%) from iron overload states such as hereditary hemochromatosis (TSAT above 45%). TSAT represents the adequacy of iron supply to erythropoietic tissue and other iron-requiring cells. Because both serum iron and transferrin vary with diurnal rhythms and acute-phase responses, TSAT should be interpreted in conjunction with ferritin and clinical context.
info.why: Diagnoses iron deficiency and hemochromatosis (iron overload).

longCommonName: Urate [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: End product of purine metabolism excreted by the kidneys.
info.scientific: Uric acid is the end product of purine nucleotide catabolism in humans, formed by the oxidation of hypoxanthine and xanthine by the enzyme xanthine oxidase. Unlike most mammals, humans lack the enzyme uricase and therefore cannot further degrade uric acid to the more soluble allantoin, leading to higher circulating levels. Hyperuricemia results from overproduction (high purine diet, cell turnover, enzymatic defects) or underexcretion (renal insufficiency, diuretics) and can lead to monosodium urate crystal deposition causing gouty arthritis, tophi, and urate nephropathy. Elevated uric acid is also independently associated with hypertension, metabolic syndrome, chronic kidney disease, and cardiovascular disease.
info.why: Diagnoses gout, monitors kidney function, and assesses cardiovascular risk.

longCommonName: Glomerular filtration rate/1.73 sq M.predicted [Volume Rate/Area] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Estimated rate at which the kidneys filter blood, calculated from creatinine and demographics.
info.scientific: Estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) is a calculated measure of kidney filtration capacity derived from serum creatinine concentration using population-based equations such as CKD-EPI (Chronic Kidney Disease Epidemiology Collaboration) that incorporate age and sex. GFR represents the total volume of plasma filtered by all functioning nephrons per unit time and is the best overall index of kidney function. The eGFR is used to stage chronic kidney disease (CKD stages 1-5), guide drug dosing for renally cleared medications, and determine eligibility for kidney replacement therapy. Limitations include inaccuracy at extremes of muscle mass, unstable creatinine states, and the use of population-derived coefficients that may not apply to all ethnic groups.
info.why: Stages chronic kidney disease and monitors kidney function over time.
info.frequency: Annually; every 3-6 months in chronic kidney disease

longCommonName: Cystatin C [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Small protein filtered by the kidneys, used as an alternative marker of kidney function.
info.scientific: Cystatin C is a 13-kDa cysteine protease inhibitor produced at a relatively constant rate by all nucleated cells and freely filtered by the glomerulus, with complete tubular reabsorption and catabolism and no tubular secretion. Unlike creatinine, cystatin C production is largely independent of muscle mass, sex, age, and diet, making it a potentially superior endogenous marker of GFR in populations where creatinine-based estimates may be unreliable. Combined creatinine-cystatin C equations (CKD-EPI 2021) provide more accurate GFR estimation than either marker alone. Cystatin C is particularly useful for confirming CKD in patients with borderline creatinine-based eGFR, in the elderly, and in those with extreme body composition.
info.why: Provides a more accurate GFR estimate, especially when creatinine may be unreliable (e.g., extreme muscle mass).

longCommonName: Activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT) in Blood

info.description: Measures the time it takes for blood to clot via the intrinsic coagulation pathway.
info.scientific: Activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT) is a coagulation screening test that measures the time required for clot formation after activation of the intrinsic pathway by a contact activator (kaolin, silica, or ellagic acid) in the presence of phospholipid and calcium. It evaluates factors XII, XI, IX, VIII, X, V, prothrombin, and fibrinogen (the intrinsic and common pathways). Prolonged aPTT may result from deficiency or inhibition of these factors, as seen in hemophilia A (factor VIII deficiency), hemophilia B (factor IX deficiency), von Willebrand disease, lupus anticoagulant, and heparin therapy. The aPTT is the standard test for monitoring unfractionated heparin therapy and screening for coagulation factor deficiencies.
info.why: Screens for clotting factor deficiencies and monitors heparin therapy.

longCommonName: INR in Platelet poor plasma by Coagulation assay

info.description: Standardized ratio of prothrombin time used to compare coagulation across laboratories.
info.scientific: The International Normalized Ratio (INR) is a standardized expression of the prothrombin time (PT) calculated using the formula: INR = (patient PT / mean normal PT)^ISI, where ISI (International Sensitivity Index) corrects for the thromboplastin reagent used. The INR was developed by the World Health Organization to eliminate inter-laboratory variability in PT reporting and enable standardized monitoring of vitamin K antagonist (warfarin) therapy. The therapeutic INR range for most indications (venous thromboembolism, atrial fibrillation) is 2.0-3.0, while mechanical heart valves may require a target of 2.5-3.5. INR elevation reflects decreased functional activity of vitamin K-dependent coagulation factors (II, VII, IX, X) and may also occur in liver disease, DIC, and factor deficiencies.
info.why: Monitors warfarin therapy and evaluates the extrinsic clotting pathway.
info.frequency: Weekly to monthly when on warfarin; otherwise as clinically indicated

longCommonName: Prothrombin time (PT) in Platelet poor plasma by Coagulation assay

info.description: Measures the time for blood to clot via the extrinsic and common coagulation pathways.
info.scientific: Prothrombin time (PT) is a coagulation screening test that measures the time required for clot formation after addition of tissue thromboplastin (tissue factor + phospholipid) and calcium to citrated platelet-poor plasma. It evaluates the extrinsic (factor VII) and common (factors X, V, prothrombin, and fibrinogen) coagulation pathways. Prolonged PT occurs in vitamin K deficiency, warfarin therapy, liver disease, disseminated intravascular coagulation, and deficiencies of factors VII, X, V, II, or fibrinogen. PT is used clinically to assess hepatic synthetic function, screen for coagulation disorders, and, when expressed as INR, to monitor warfarin anticoagulation therapy.
info.why: Evaluates bleeding risk, liver function, and monitors anticoagulant therapy.

longCommonName: Fibrin D-dimer FEU [Mass/volume] in Platelet poor plasma

info.description: Fibrin degradation product released when blood clots break down.
info.scientific: D-dimer is a fibrin degradation product consisting of two cross-linked D fragments released when plasmin cleaves cross-linked (stabilized) fibrin in a thrombus. Its presence in plasma indicates that both thrombin generation (coagulation) and plasmin generation (fibrinolysis) have occurred. D-dimer has high sensitivity but low specificity for venous thromboembolism (VTE), making it most clinically useful as a rule-out test: a normal D-dimer in conjunction with a low clinical probability score effectively excludes deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism. Elevated D-dimer levels also occur in disseminated intravascular coagulation, recent surgery, trauma, infection, malignancy, pregnancy, and liver disease.
info.why: Rules out deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism; also monitors clotting disorders.

longCommonName: Prothrombin time (PT) in Platelet poor plasma by Coagulation assay --percent of normal

info.description: Prothrombin time expressed as a percentage of a normal reference value.
info.scientific: Prothrombin index (PI) expresses the prothrombin time as a percentage of normal, calculated as (normal PT / patient PT) x 100, providing an intuitive measure of extrinsic and common coagulation pathway function. A higher percentage indicates faster clotting and more intact coagulation function. The prothrombin index is used in some clinical settings and laboratory traditions (particularly in European and post-Soviet medical practice) as an alternative or complement to the INR for assessing coagulation status. Decreased PI (below reference range) indicates hypocoagulability from vitamin K deficiency, warfarin therapy, hepatic synthetic dysfunction, or factor deficiencies.
info.why: Assesses extrinsic coagulation pathway function and liver synthetic capacity.

longCommonName: Thrombin time in Platelet poor plasma by Coagulation assay

info.description: Measures the time for fibrinogen to convert to fibrin in the final step of clot formation.
info.scientific: Thrombin time (TT) measures the time for clot formation after exogenous thrombin is added directly to citrated plasma, evaluating the final step of the coagulation cascade: the conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin. It bypasses all upstream coagulation factors and is specifically sensitive to fibrinogen quantity and quality, the presence of fibrin degradation products (FDPs), heparin, and direct thrombin inhibitors. Prolonged thrombin time occurs in hypofibrinogenemia, dysfibrinogenemia, elevated FDPs (as in DIC), heparin therapy, and the presence of direct thrombin inhibitors (dabigatran). The TT is a useful supplementary test for evaluating unexplained prolongation of PT or aPTT and for detecting heparin contamination in blood samples.
info.why: Detects fibrinogen abnormalities, heparin contamination, and fibrin degradation products.

longCommonName: Antithrombin [Enzymatic activity/volume] in Platelet poor plasma

info.description: Natural anticoagulant protein that inactivates thrombin and other clotting factors.
info.scientific: Antithrombin (formerly antithrombin III) is a serine protease inhibitor (serpin) synthesized by the liver that is the most important natural anticoagulant, accounting for approximately 80% of thrombin inhibition in vivo. It inactivates thrombin and factors Xa, IXa, XIa, and XIIa by forming irreversible complexes, a process dramatically accelerated (up to 1000-fold) by heparin binding. Hereditary antithrombin deficiency (prevalence ~0.02-0.2%) is the most thrombogenic of the inherited thrombophilias, with a lifetime thrombotic risk of 50-85%. Acquired deficiency occurs in liver disease, DIC, nephrotic syndrome, and L-asparaginase therapy, and causes heparin resistance when antithrombin levels are critically low.
info.why: Identifies antithrombin deficiency as a cause of recurrent venous thrombosis.

longCommonName: Erythrocyte sedimentation rate

info.description: Non-specific measure of how fast red blood cells settle in a tube, indicating inflammation.
info.scientific: Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) measures the rate at which erythrocytes settle in a vertical column of anticoagulated whole blood over one hour, expressed in millimeters. The rate of sedimentation is primarily determined by the degree of rouleaux formation, which increases when acute-phase proteins (particularly fibrinogen and immunoglobulins) neutralize the negative surface charge of red blood cells. ESR is a non-specific marker of inflammation used to screen for, diagnose, and monitor inflammatory and infectious conditions including temporal arteritis, polymyalgia rheumatica, rheumatoid arthritis, and osteomyelitis. ESR is influenced by age, sex, anemia, and pregnancy, and has largely been complemented by CRP for acute inflammatory monitoring due to CRP's faster response kinetics.
info.why: Screens for and monitors inflammatory conditions such as infections and autoimmune diseases.

longCommonName: Corticotropin [Mass/volume] in Plasma

info.description: Pituitary hormone that stimulates the adrenal glands to produce cortisol.
info.scientific: Adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) is a 39-amino acid polypeptide cleaved from the precursor proopiomelanocortin (POMC) and secreted by corticotroph cells of the anterior pituitary gland in a pulsatile, circadian pattern (peak in early morning). ACTH stimulates the adrenal cortex to synthesize and secrete cortisol (zona fasciculata), androgens (zona reticularis), and to a lesser extent aldosterone. ACTH measurement is essential for differentiating ACTH-dependent (pituitary adenoma, ectopic ACTH) from ACTH-independent (adrenal tumor, exogenous glucocorticoid) causes of Cushing syndrome, and for evaluating primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease, elevated ACTH) versus secondary adrenal insufficiency (low ACTH). ACTH requires careful specimen handling (immediate cooling, EDTA plasma) due to its short half-life and instability.
info.why: Differentiates pituitary from adrenal causes of cortisol disorders (Cushing's, Addison's).

longCommonName: Cortisol [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Primary stress hormone produced by the adrenal glands that regulates metabolism and immune response.
info.scientific: Cortisol is the principal endogenous glucocorticoid steroid hormone synthesized from cholesterol in the zona fasciculata of the adrenal cortex under the regulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis. It exhibits a pronounced circadian rhythm with peak levels in the early morning and a nadir at midnight. Cortisol has widespread physiological effects including stimulation of gluconeogenesis, suppression of inflammatory and immune responses, maintenance of vascular tone, and stress adaptation. Elevated cortisol (Cushing syndrome) causes central obesity, hyperglycemia, hypertension, osteoporosis, and immune suppression, while cortisol deficiency (Addison disease, secondary adrenal insufficiency) presents with fatigue, hypotension, hyponatremia, and potentially life-threatening adrenal crisis.
info.why: Diagnoses adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease) and cortisol excess (Cushing's syndrome).

longCommonName: Rheumatoid factor [Units/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Autoantibody directed against the Fc portion of IgG, often elevated in autoimmune conditions.
info.scientific: Rheumatoid factor (RF) is an autoantibody, predominantly of the IgM class, directed against the Fc region of IgG immunoglobulins. It is produced by B lymphocytes and plasma cells in the synovium and lymphoid tissue and forms immune complexes that activate complement, perpetuating chronic inflammatory synovitis. RF is present in approximately 70-80% of patients with rheumatoid arthritis (seropositive RA) and is one of the classification criteria for the disease. However, RF lacks specificity as it may also be elevated in other autoimmune conditions (Sjogren syndrome, SLE), chronic infections (hepatitis C, endocarditis), and in 5-10% of healthy elderly individuals.
info.why: Supports diagnosis of rheumatoid arthritis and other autoimmune diseases.

longCommonName: Insulin-like growth factor-I [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Growth factor mediating the effects of growth hormone on tissue growth and metabolism.
info.scientific: Insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1, somatomedin C) is a 70-amino acid polypeptide produced primarily by hepatocytes in response to growth hormone (GH) stimulation, mediating most of the anabolic and growth-promoting effects of GH through endocrine, paracrine, and autocrine mechanisms. IGF-1 circulates bound to IGF-binding proteins (predominantly IGFBP-3) and acts through the IGF-1 receptor (a tyrosine kinase) to stimulate cell proliferation, differentiation, and inhibit apoptosis. IGF-1 measurement serves as a surrogate marker for integrated GH secretion because it has a long half-life and stable circulating levels, unlike GH which is secreted in a pulsatile pattern. It is used to screen for GH deficiency (low IGF-1) and GH excess/acromegaly (elevated IGF-1), and to monitor the response to GH replacement or suppressive therapy.
info.why: Screens for growth hormone deficiency or excess (acromegaly) and monitors treatment.
info.frequency: As clinically indicated; every 6-12 months if monitoring therapy

longCommonName: Prostate specific Ag [Mass/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Protein produced by the prostate gland, elevated in prostate cancer and benign prostate conditions.
info.scientific: Prostate-specific antigen (PSA) is a 33-kDa serine protease (kallikrein-related peptidase 3, KLK3) produced almost exclusively by prostatic epithelial cells, where it liquefies seminal fluid by cleaving semenogelin. In serum, PSA circulates in free and complexed (primarily with alpha-1-antichymotrypsin) forms. PSA is not cancer-specific; levels are elevated in prostate cancer, benign prostatic hyperplasia, prostatitis, and after prostatic manipulation. PSA is used for prostate cancer screening, staging, and monitoring treatment response, with a traditional threshold of 4.0 ng/mL, though cancer can occur at lower levels. Free-to-total PSA ratio, PSA density, and PSA velocity improve the specificity of screening.
info.why: Screens for prostate cancer and monitors treatment response in diagnosed patients.
info.frequency: Annually for men over 50 (or over 40 if high risk); as clinically indicated

longCommonName: Cyclic citrullinated peptide Ab [Units/volume] in Serum

info.description: Highly specific autoantibody associated with rheumatoid arthritis.
info.scientific: Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are autoantibodies directed against proteins in which arginine residues have been post-translationally converted to citrulline by peptidylarginine deiminase (PAD) enzymes. These antibodies have high specificity (approximately 95-98%) for rheumatoid arthritis and can be detected years before clinical onset, making them valuable for early diagnosis. Anti-CCP antibodies are associated with more aggressive erosive joint disease and extra-articular manifestations, providing important prognostic information. Combined with rheumatoid factor, anti-CCP testing forms part of the 2010 ACR/EULAR classification criteria for rheumatoid arthritis and helps distinguish RA from other inflammatory arthritides.
info.why: Confirms rheumatoid arthritis diagnosis with high specificity and predicts disease severity.
info.frequency: Once for diagnosis; repeat only as clinically indicated

longCommonName: Creatine kinase [Enzymatic activity/volume] in Serum or Plasma

info.description: Enzyme found in muscle tissue released into the blood when muscles are damaged.
info.scientific: Creatine kinase (CK) is a dimeric enzyme catalyzing the reversible transfer of phosphate from creatine phosphate to ADP, generating ATP for energy-requiring cellular processes. Three CK isoenzymes exist: CK-MM (skeletal muscle, ~95% of serum CK), CK-MB (cardiac muscle), and CK-BB (brain). Serum CK elevation primarily reflects skeletal muscle damage and is markedly elevated in rhabdomyolysis, muscular dystrophies, inflammatory myopathies, and after intense exercise. CK-MB was historically used as a cardiac biomarker for myocardial infarction but has been largely superseded by troponin assays. CK measurement is also important for monitoring statin-associated myopathy and evaluating neuromuscular diseases.
info.why: Detects muscle injury, rhabdomyolysis, and monitors statin-related myopathy.

longCommonName: Neutrophils [#/volume] in Blood by Automated count

info.description: The front-line soldiers of your immune system — they arrive first at any site of infection and destroy bacteria by engulfing them. Your bone marrow pumps out billions daily, and they live only hours in the bloodstream before migrating into tissues. A spike usually means a bacterial infection; a crash can leave you vulnerable to life-threatening infections.
info.scientific: Neutrophils are the most abundant circulating leukocytes (40-70% of WBC), derived from granulocyte-monocyte progenitors in the bone marrow through granulopoiesis regulated by granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF). They are the primary effector cells of innate immunity, rapidly recruited to sites of infection where they phagocytose and kill bacteria and fungi through oxygen-dependent (respiratory burst, myeloperoxidase) and oxygen-independent (defensins, lysozyme) mechanisms, and by releasing neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs). Neutrophilia (elevated count) is the hallmark of bacterial infection, acute inflammation, stress, and corticosteroid use, while neutropenia (decreased count) increases susceptibility to life-threatening infections and may result from bone marrow suppression, autoimmune destruction, or congenital disorders.
info.why: Detects bacterial infections, monitors chemotherapy effects, and evaluates immune status.

longCommonName: Lymphocytes [#/volume] in Blood by Automated count

info.description: The intelligence branch of your immune system — they remember every pathogen you've ever fought. B-lymphocytes produce antibodies that tag invaders for destruction, while T-lymphocytes coordinate attacks and kill infected cells directly. High counts often point to viral infections; persistently low counts weaken your body's ability to fight off disease.
info.scientific: Lymphocytes are mononuclear leukocytes that mediate adaptive immunity and comprise T cells (~70-80%, cell-mediated immunity), B cells (~10-15%, humoral immunity and antibody production), and natural killer (NK) cells (~5-10%, innate cytotoxicity). They originate from common lymphoid progenitors in the bone marrow, with T cells maturing in the thymus and B cells completing maturation in the marrow. Lymphocytosis is characteristic of viral infections, chronic lymphocytic leukemia, and pertussis, while lymphopenia occurs in HIV/AIDS, corticosteroid therapy, autoimmune diseases, and severe systemic illness. The absolute lymphocyte count is a critical component of the complete blood count differential and is essential for evaluating immune competence.
info.why: Evaluates viral infections, immune deficiencies, and lymphoproliferative disorders.

longCommonName: Monocytes [#/volume] in Blood by Automated count

info.description: The cleanup crew — these large cells patrol your blood, then migrate into tissues where they transform into macrophages that swallow bacteria, dead cells, and debris. They also act as messengers, presenting pieces of invaders to lymphocytes so the immune system can mount a precise response. Elevated counts can signal chronic infection, inflammation, or autoimmune conditions.
info.scientific: Monocytes are the largest of the circulating leukocytes, produced in the bone marrow from granulocyte-monocyte progenitors and released into the blood where they circulate for 1-3 days before migrating into tissues to differentiate into macrophages, dendritic cells, and osteoclasts. As professional phagocytes, they play crucial roles in innate immunity (pathogen recognition and destruction), antigen presentation to T cells (bridging innate and adaptive immunity), tissue remodeling, and wound healing. Monocytosis is associated with chronic infections (tuberculosis, endocarditis), autoimmune and granulomatous diseases, myeloproliferative disorders (chronic myelomonocytic leukemia), and recovery from bone marrow suppression. Monocyte counts are an important component of the differential white blood cell count.
info.why: Helps diagnose chronic infections, inflammatory conditions, and blood disorders.

longCommonName: Eosinophils [#/volume] in Blood by Automated count

info.description: Specialist fighters tuned to two threats — parasites and allergic reactions. They release toxic granules that destroy parasitic worms too large for other cells to engulf, but the same chemicals also drive allergic inflammation. A rise often points to allergies, asthma, or parasitic infection; they're a window into your body's hypersensitivity responses.
info.scientific: Eosinophils are granulocytic leukocytes characterized by bilobed nuclei and large cytoplasmic granules containing major basic protein (MBP), eosinophil cationic protein (ECP), eosinophil peroxidase, and eosinophil-derived neurotoxin. They develop in the bone marrow under the influence of IL-3, IL-5, and GM-CSF, and primarily reside in tissues (gut, respiratory tract). Eosinophils play key roles in defense against helminthic parasites, modulation of allergic inflammation, and tissue remodeling, but their cytotoxic granule contents can also cause tissue damage. Eosinophilia is characteristic of allergic diseases (asthma, atopic dermatitis), parasitic infections, drug reactions, and hypereosinophilic syndromes.
info.why: Detects allergic conditions, parasitic infections, and eosinophilic disorders.

longCommonName: Basophils [#/volume] in Blood by Automated count

info.description: The rarest white blood cells, but powerful alarm-pullers — they release histamine and other chemicals that dilate blood vessels and attract other immune cells to the scene. They play a key role in severe allergic reactions and anaphylaxis. Usually present in tiny numbers, a significant rise can signal allergic disorders, certain blood cancers, or chronic inflammation.
info.scientific: Basophils are the rarest circulating granulocytes (<1% of WBC), distinguished by large metachromatic granules containing histamine, heparin, and leukotrienes, and by high-affinity IgE receptors (FcepsilonRI) on their surface. They play a central role in IgE-mediated allergic reactions: cross-linking of surface IgE by allergens triggers immediate degranulation, releasing vasoactive mediators that cause bronchoconstriction, vasodilation, and increased vascular permeability. Basophils also produce IL-4 and IL-13, promoting Th2 immune polarization and IgE class switching. Basophilia is associated with allergic reactions, chronic myeloid leukemia, myeloproliferative neoplasms, and hypothyroidism.
info.why: Helps evaluate allergic conditions and myeloproliferative disorders.

longCommonName: Neutrophils/100 leukocytes in Blood by Automated count

info.description: Shows what share of your white blood cell army is made up of neutrophils — the bacteria-fighting front line. Together with the absolute count, the percentage reveals whether a shift in your immune balance is real or just reflects a change in total white cell numbers. A high percentage with high WBC strongly suggests bacterial infection.
info.scientific: Neutrophil percentage represents the proportion of neutrophils relative to total leukocytes in the white blood cell differential count. It is derived from automated hematology analyzers using light scatter, impedance, and cytochemical staining characteristics to differentiate cell populations. While the absolute neutrophil count is generally more clinically meaningful, the percentage provides context for interpreting the differential pattern. A left shift (increased band forms or immature neutrophils) may be present even with a normal percentage, indicating acute bacterial infection or marrow stress.
info.why: Helps interpret the differential white blood cell count for infection or bone marrow assessment.

longCommonName: Lymphocytes/100 leukocytes in Blood by Automated count

info.description: The proportion of your white cells devoted to long-term immunity — the memory and precision-targeting branch. A high percentage often indicates viral infections, since lymphocytes are the main fighters against viruses. Comparing the percentage to the absolute count helps distinguish true lymphocyte changes from shifts caused by other cell types rising or falling.
info.scientific: Lymphocyte percentage represents the proportion of lymphocytes relative to total leukocytes in the peripheral blood differential count. In healthy adults, lymphocytes typically constitute 20-40% of circulating white blood cells. Relative lymphocytosis (increased percentage) may reflect either true lymphocyte expansion (viral infections, chronic lymphocytic leukemia) or a relative increase due to neutropenia. Relative lymphopenia (decreased percentage) can occur with neutrophilia shifting the differential or from true lymphocyte depletion in HIV, corticosteroid therapy, or immunosuppressive treatment.
info.why: Aids in diagnosing viral infections, immune deficiencies, and lymphocytic disorders.

longCommonName: Monocytes/100 leukocytes in Blood by Automated count

info.description: The share of your immune cells that are monocytes — the cleanup and communication crew. A rising percentage can point to chronic infections, autoimmune conditions, or recovery phases after acute illness. Read alongside the absolute count to tell if monocytes are truly increasing or just appear higher because other cell types dropped.
info.scientific: Monocyte percentage represents the proportion of monocytes relative to total leukocytes in the white blood cell differential count, normally comprising 2-8% of circulating leukocytes. This percentage is determined by automated hematology analyzers using cell size, nuclear characteristics, and cytoplasmic properties. An elevated monocyte percentage may indicate chronic infections (tuberculosis, subacute bacterial endocarditis), autoimmune diseases, myeloproliferative disorders, or recovery from acute infection or bone marrow suppression. The monocyte percentage should always be interpreted alongside the absolute monocyte count and other differential components for accurate clinical assessment.
info.why: Helps evaluate chronic infections, inflammatory conditions, and hematologic disorders.

longCommonName: Eosinophils/100 leukocytes in Blood by Automated count

info.description: What fraction of your white cells are the allergy-and-parasite specialists. Even small percentage increases can be clinically meaningful, since eosinophils are normally a tiny minority. A high share often signals allergic conditions, asthma, or parasitic infections.
info.scientific: Eosinophil percentage represents the proportion of eosinophils relative to total leukocytes in the peripheral blood differential count, normally comprising 1-4% of circulating white blood cells. It is determined by automated hematology analyzers that identify eosinophils by their characteristic granule staining and light scatter properties. An elevated eosinophil percentage may indicate allergic diseases, parasitic infections, drug hypersensitivity, or eosinophilic disorders, though the absolute eosinophil count is more clinically reliable for defining eosinophilia. Persistent eosinophilia warrants investigation for underlying causes including hypereosinophilic syndromes and eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis.
info.why: Screens for allergic reactions, parasitic infections, and eosinophilic disorders.

longCommonName: Basophils/100 leukocytes in Blood by Automated count

info.description: The tiniest slice of your white cell population — basophils are so rare that even 1-2% is considered elevated. Because their numbers are so small, percentage changes are less reliable than absolute counts. A genuine rise can point to allergic reactions, certain blood disorders, or chronic inflammation.
info.scientific: Basophil percentage represents the proportion of basophils relative to total leukocytes in the peripheral blood differential count, normally comprising less than 1% of circulating white blood cells. Basophils are the least numerous granulocytes and are identified by automated analyzers based on their unique staining and scatter characteristics. An elevated basophil percentage may suggest allergic reactions, chronic myeloid leukemia (where basophilia can be an early sign), other myeloproliferative neoplasms, hypothyroidism, or inflammatory bowel disease. Due to their very low numbers, even small absolute increases can cause a notable percentage change.
info.why: Helps evaluate allergic conditions and chronic myeloproliferative disorders.

longCommonName: Mean corpuscular volume [Entitic volume] by Automated count

info.description: How big your red blood cells are on average — one of the most important clues for diagnosing the type of anemia. Small cells (low MCV) usually point to iron deficiency; large cells (high MCV) suggest vitamin B12 or folate deficiency. Normal-sized cells with low hemoglobin point to chronic disease or blood loss.
info.scientific: Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) is the average volume of individual erythrocytes, calculated by dividing the hematocrit by the red blood cell count and expressed in femtoliters (fL). It is the primary parameter for morphologically classifying anemias: microcytic (MCV < 80 fL, as in iron deficiency and thalassemia), normocytic (MCV 80-100 fL, as in anemia of chronic disease and acute blood loss), and macrocytic (MCV > 100 fL, as in vitamin B12/folate deficiency and myelodysplastic syndromes). MCV is measured directly by automated hematology analyzers using impedance or light scatter methods. MCV may be spuriously elevated by cold agglutinins, hyperglycemia (osmotic cell swelling), or marked reticulocytosis.
info.why: Classifies anemia as microcytic, normocytic, or macrocytic to guide diagnosis.

longCommonName: Mean corpuscular hemoglobin [Entitic mass] by Automated count

info.description: How much oxygen-carrying hemoglobin each red blood cell is loaded with — a measure of how well each cell is equipped for its job. Low MCH means cells are under-loaded (often from iron deficiency), producing pale, weak cells. High MCH means cells are oversized and overstuffed, typically from B12 or folate problems.
info.scientific: Mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH) is the average mass of hemoglobin per individual red blood cell, calculated by dividing the hemoglobin concentration by the red blood cell count and expressed in picograms (pg). MCH generally parallels MCV, with low values (hypochromia) occurring in iron deficiency anemia and thalassemia, and elevated values in macrocytic anemias from B12 or folate deficiency. MCH provides information about the hemoglobin content of average erythrocytes and is useful for classifying anemias alongside MCV and MCHC. MCH may be spuriously affected by lipemia, hyperbilirubinemia, and extremely high white blood cell counts that interfere with hemoglobin measurement.
info.why: Helps classify anemia types and complements MCV in evaluating red cell disorders.

longCommonName: Mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration [Mass/volume] by Automated count

info.description: How densely packed the hemoglobin is inside each red blood cell — not just how much, but how concentrated. Low MCHC produces pale, "washed-out" cells (hypochromic), classic in iron deficiency. High MCHC is rare and can indicate spherocytosis, where cells lose their disc shape and become small, dense spheres.
info.scientific: Mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC) is the average concentration of hemoglobin within red blood cells, calculated by dividing hemoglobin by hematocrit and expressed in g/dL. It reflects the ratio of hemoglobin mass to red cell volume and is the most specific red cell index for detecting hypochromia. Low MCHC indicates hypochromic red cells as seen in iron deficiency anemia and thalassemia trait. Elevated MCHC (hyperchromia) is characteristic of hereditary spherocytosis (where cell volume is reduced while hemoglobin content is preserved) and may also be seen as a laboratory artifact in the presence of cold agglutinins or severe lipemia.
info.why: Detects hypochromic anemias such as iron deficiency and hereditary spherocytosis.

longCommonName: Erythrocyte distribution width [Ratio] by Automated count

info.description: How much your red blood cells vary in size — like checking whether a batch of manufactured parts is uniform or all over the place. A high RDW means your bone marrow is producing cells of very different sizes, which is an early sign of iron, B12, or folate deficiency — often rising before hemoglobin drops. It's one of the earliest warning signals of developing anemia.
info.scientific: Red cell distribution width coefficient of variation (RDW-CV) is a quantitative measure of the heterogeneity of red blood cell volumes (anisocytosis), calculated as the standard deviation of red cell volumes divided by MCV, expressed as a percentage. Normal values are typically 11.5-14.5%. Elevated RDW-CV indicates increased variability in red cell size and is a hallmark of iron deficiency anemia, where a mixed population of microcytic and normocytic cells coexists. RDW-CV is particularly useful for differentiating iron deficiency anemia (elevated RDW) from thalassemia trait (normal RDW), both of which present with low MCV. Recent evidence also associates elevated RDW with cardiovascular disease, heart failure, and increased all-cause mortality.
info.why: Helps differentiate anemia types; elevated in iron deficiency and mixed nutritional anemias.

longCommonName: Erythrocyte distribution width [Entitic volume] by Automated count

info.description: Another way to measure red blood cell size variation, but in absolute units rather than as a percentage. It's more sensitive than RDW-CV at detecting two distinct populations of cells — for example, a mix of normal and abnormally large cells. This makes it particularly useful for catching early nutritional deficiencies or monitoring response to treatment.
info.scientific: Red cell distribution width standard deviation (RDW-SD) is an absolute measure of the dispersion of red blood cell volumes, expressed in femtoliters (fL), derived directly from the width of the erythrocyte volume distribution histogram at the 20% frequency level. Unlike RDW-CV which is a coefficient of variation normalized to MCV, RDW-SD provides a volume-independent measure of anisocytosis. RDW-SD is less affected by MCV changes, making it more sensitive for detecting early anisocytosis in macrocytic conditions. Elevated RDW-SD reflects the presence of red cell populations of disparate sizes (anisocytosis), aiding in the classification and differential diagnosis of anemias.
info.why: Provides a direct measure of red cell size variability to help classify anemias.

longCommonName: Platelets [#/volume] in Blood by Automated count

info.description: Tiny cell fragments that act as your body's emergency repair patches — they rush to any break in a blood vessel, clump together, and form a plug to stop bleeding. Your body maintains a huge reserve because rapid blood loss demands an instant response. Too few platelets risk dangerous bleeding; too many increase the chance of unwanted clots.
info.scientific: Platelets (thrombocytes) are small, anucleate cell fragments derived from megakaryocytes in the bone marrow through a process regulated by thrombopoietin. They play a central role in primary hemostasis by adhering to damaged endothelium (via von Willebrand factor and glycoprotein Ib), activating and aggregating to form a platelet plug, and providing a phospholipid surface for coagulation factor assembly. Platelets also release granule contents (ADP, thromboxane A2, serotonin, growth factors) that amplify the hemostatic response and promote wound healing. Thrombocytopenia (low count) increases bleeding risk, while thrombocytosis (elevated count) may be reactive or indicate a myeloproliferative neoplasm.
info.why: Detects thrombocytopenia or thrombocytosis and evaluates bleeding or clotting risk.

longCommonName: Mean platelet volume [Entitic volume] in Blood by Automated count

info.description: The average size of your platelets — a window into how hard your bone marrow is working. Young, freshly made platelets are larger and more active, so a high MPV means your marrow is ramping up production, often to replace platelets being consumed. A rising MPV with falling platelet count is a sign the body is destroying or using up platelets faster than it can make them.
info.scientific: Mean platelet volume (MPV) is the average size of circulating platelets, measured in femtoliters (fL) by automated hematology analyzers. MPV reflects thrombopoietic activity, with larger platelets being younger, more metabolically active, and containing more granules and thromboxane A2 than smaller, older platelets. Elevated MPV is associated with increased platelet turnover states such as immune thrombocytopenia (ITP), where the bone marrow compensates for peripheral destruction by releasing larger, younger platelets. Decreased MPV may indicate hypoproductive thrombocytopenia from bone marrow failure. MPV has also been investigated as a cardiovascular risk marker, with higher values associated with acute myocardial infarction and stroke.
info.why: Helps distinguish causes of abnormal platelet counts (production vs. destruction).

longCommonName: Platelet distribution width [Entitic volume] in Blood by Automated count

info.description: How much your platelets vary in size — similar to what RDW does for red blood cells. A wide distribution means your bone marrow is releasing platelets of different ages and sizes, which can indicate active platelet turnover or production stress. It helps distinguish between different causes of abnormal platelet counts.
info.scientific: Platelet distribution width (PDW) quantifies the variation in platelet size within the blood, analogous to RDW for red blood cells. It is derived from the platelet volume distribution curve generated by automated hematology analyzers. Increased PDW indicates greater heterogeneity in platelet size, reflecting active thrombopoiesis with a mix of newly released large platelets and older smaller ones. PDW is useful for differentiating reactive thrombocytosis (usually normal PDW) from myeloproliferative disorders (often increased PDW). It has also been studied as an inflammatory marker and has shown associations with various conditions including sepsis, acute coronary syndromes, and malignancies.
info.why: Aids in differentiating reactive thrombocytosis from myeloproliferative disorders.

longCommonName: Platelet large cell ratio in Blood by Automated count

info.description: What proportion of your platelets are oversized — large platelets are young, fresh from the bone marrow, and more "sticky" (reactive) than smaller, older ones. A high ratio signals the marrow is actively pushing out new platelets, often in response to increased destruction or consumption. It's a useful complement to MPV for understanding platelet turnover.
info.scientific: Platelet large cell ratio (P-LCR) is the percentage of platelets exceeding a defined volume threshold (typically >12 fL), measured by automated hematology analyzers. A higher P-LCR indicates a greater proportion of large, young, more reactive platelets in circulation, suggesting increased platelet turnover or accelerated thrombopoiesis. Elevated P-LCR is observed in immune thrombocytopenia (where peripheral destruction stimulates compensatory megakaryopoiesis), myeloproliferative disorders, and inflammatory conditions. P-LCR is clinically useful alongside MPV and PDW for differentiating consumptive from hypoproductive causes of thrombocytopenia. It has also been investigated as a marker of platelet activation in cardiovascular disease.
info.why: Indicates increased platelet turnover and helps evaluate thrombocytopenic conditions.

longCommonName: Platelet large cells [#/volume] in Blood by Automated count

info.description: The actual number of oversized platelets in your blood, not just the percentage. Large platelets are younger, more metabolically active, and carry more clotting factors — so a high count of them means greater clotting potential. This marker adds precision to P-LCR by giving a concrete number rather than a ratio.
info.scientific: Platelet large cell count (P-LCC) is the absolute number of large platelets (typically >12 fL) per unit volume of blood, providing a quantitative complement to the P-LCR percentage. It is measured by automated hematology analyzers and reflects the absolute burden of large, immature, and more metabolically active platelets in circulation. An elevated P-LCC indicates increased thrombopoietic activity, as newly released platelets from megakaryocytes tend to be larger. P-LCC is useful in conjunction with other platelet indices (MPV, PDW, P-LCR) for evaluating the etiology of thrombocytopenia and assessing thrombopoietic reserve.
info.why: Supplements P-LCR to evaluate platelet production and destruction disorders.

longCommonName: Plateletcrit [Volume Fraction] in Blood by Automated count

info.description: The platelet equivalent of hematocrit — what fraction of your blood volume is made up of platelets. It combines platelet count and size into a single number, giving a more complete picture of total platelet mass than count alone. Useful because someone with fewer but larger platelets may have the same clotting capacity as someone with many small ones.
info.scientific: Plateletcrit (PCT) is the volume fraction of whole blood occupied by platelets, analogous to hematocrit for red blood cells, calculated as the product of platelet count and mean platelet volume divided by 10,000 and expressed as a percentage. It provides an integrated measure of total platelet mass that accounts for both the number and size of circulating platelets. Plateletcrit is useful for assessing overall platelet hemostatic capacity, particularly when platelet count and MPV change in opposite directions (as in ITP, where count decreases but MPV increases). Abnormal plateletcrit values may indicate increased bleeding or thrombotic risk and are used alongside other platelet indices for comprehensive evaluation of platelet disorders.
info.why: Provides an overall measure of platelet mass to evaluate bleeding and clotting risk.
